children.name: Phylloscopus
children.description: Leaf warblers — small, active, largely green or yellow-green warblers that glean insects from foliage with remarkable agility. The willow warbler is the single most numerous summer bird in Sweden, estimated at over 40 million breeding pairs across Scandinavia. Despite weighing just 9 grams, it completes an annual round trip of 10,000–14,000 km to sub-Saharan Africa and back. The genus spans the entire Palearctic from Atlantic Europe to the Pacific, with diversity peaking in the Himalayas and China.

children.name: Phylloscopus trochilus
children.description: The lövsångare — Sweden's most numerous summer bird with an estimated 40+ million pairs breeding across Scandinavia. Its cascading, descending song is the defining sound of Swedish spring, heard from every birch woodland to Arctic fell. Weighs just 9 g but migrates annually to sub-Saharan Africa and back.

children.name: Phylloscopus collybita
children.description: One of Europe's earliest spring arrivals; its repetitive onomatopoeic 'chiff-chaff' song is unmistakeable and often heard before the bird is seen. Almost identical to the willow warbler but reliably told by dark legs and its persistent two-note song. Increasingly overwintering in southern Europe and England.

children.name: Phylloscopus sibilatrix
children.description: The largest and most vividly coloured leaf warbler in Europe — yellow-green above, brilliant white below with a yellow supercilium. Produces two distinct songs: a silvery accelerating trill and a series of plaintive 'piu' notes. A bird of mature deciduous woodland with sparse understory; declining across northern Europe.

children.name: Phylloscopus borealis
children.description: Breeds in mountain birch forest from northern Scandinavia to Alaska — the northernmost leaf warbler in the world. Undertakes one of the longest migrations of any passerine, wintering in Southeast Asia. A large, robust leaf warbler with a bold supercilium and rasping song.

children.name: Phylloscopus abietinus
children.description: A pale, cold-toned chiffchaff breeding in boreal spruce and birch forests from Scandinavia east to western Siberia. Long treated as a subspecies of Common Chiffchaff but distinct in plumage and genetics. Winters in northeast Africa, mainly Ethiopia and Sudan.

children.name: Phylloscopus affinis
children.description: A plain, buff-washed leaf warbler of Himalayan scrub and open juniper forest from Pakistan to western China. Named after Samuel Tickell, a 19th-century British army officer and naturalist who collected widely in India. Winters in the foothills and lowlands of the Indian subcontinent.

children.name: Phylloscopus armandii
children.description: A distinctive Himalayan leaf warbler with prominent pale yellowish streaking on the upperparts and a long, full tail. Named after the French missionary and naturalist Armand David, who collected extensively in China in the 1860s. Breeds in rhododendron scrub and dwarf juniper at high altitude.

children.name: Phylloscopus bonelli
children.description: A clean, pale leaf warbler of European mountain forests — white below contrasting with a greenish-yellow rump. Named after Franco Andrea Bonelli, the Italian ornithologist who first described it in the early 19th century. Winters in sub-Saharan West Africa; closely related to the eastern counterpart P. orientalis.

children.name: Phylloscopus borealoides
children.description: An enigmatic leaf warbler breeding on Sakhalin Island and adjacent Russian Far East, formerly lumped with the Arctic Warbler complex. Split as a full species following bioacoustic and genetic work revealing marked differences in song and mitochondrial DNA. Winters in Southeast Asia.

children.name: Phylloscopus cantator
children.description: A striking Himalayan leaf warbler with bold yellow underparts and a distinctive yellow vent, differing sharply from most of its drab relatives. Breeds in subtropical broadleaf forest from the eastern Himalayas to southwest China. Its loud, melodious song is a prominent feature of montane forest soundscapes.

children.name: Phylloscopus chloronotus
children.description: A tiny, active Himalayan leaf warbler with a diagnostic lemon-yellow rump patch that flashes conspicuously in flight. Breeds in high-altitude fir and rhododendron forest from the Himalayas to western China. Often forages in mixed-species flocks alongside other small Phylloscopus warblers.

children.name: Phylloscopus coronatus
children.description: A distinctive East Asian leaf warbler with a bright yellow crown stripe set between dark lateral stripes, giving a crowned appearance. Breeds in deciduous and mixed forest from Siberia south to Korea and Japan. A scarce but regular vagrant to Western Europe; winters in tropical Southeast Asia.

children.name: Phylloscopus emeiensis
children.description: A recently described species restricted to broadleaf forest on and around Mount Emei in Sichuan, China, separated from the Blyth's Leaf Warbler by song analysis and morphology. One of several cryptic species revealed by bioacoustic surveys of the Chinese mountain avifauna in the 2000s–2010s. Resident or short-distance migrant.

children.name: Phylloscopus fuscatus
children.description: A skulking, drab brown leaf warbler that creeps mouse-like through low vegetation, quite unlike the aerial-gleaning style of most relatives. Breeds in boggy taiga from Siberia to northeast China; winters in South and Southeast Asia. A scarce but annually recorded vagrant in Western Europe, mostly in autumn.

children.name: Phylloscopus fuligiventer
children.description: One of the darkest and most soot-coloured leaf warblers, with dusky brown underparts setting it apart from almost all congeners. Breeds in dense scrub along mountain streams at high altitude in the Himalayas and Tibetan plateau. Forages low in damp thickets, often near rushing watercourses.

children.name: Phylloscopus griseolus
children.description: A compact, rather short-tailed leaf warbler of rocky hillsides with sparse vegetation in the western Himalayas and adjacent ranges. Its sulphur-yellow underparts and grey-brown upperparts are distinctive. Winters in the Indian lowlands; a common altitudinal migrant on the northwestern Himalayan front.

children.name: Phylloscopus hainanus
children.description: An endemic leaf warbler restricted to montane broadleaf forests of Hainan Island, southern China. Separated from closely related crown-striped warblers by song differences and subtle morphological characters. One of several endemic bird species confined to the biodiversity hotspot of Hainan's central highlands.

children.name: Phylloscopus humei
children.description: A pale, cold-toned leaf warbler of high Himalayan and Central Asian mountain forests, named after the pioneering Indian ornithologist Allan Octavian Hume. Split from Yellow-browed Warbler in the 1990s following molecular and vocal studies. Winters across lowland South Asia from Pakistan to Bangladesh.

children.name: Phylloscopus ibericus
children.description: A recently recognised full species breeding on the Iberian Peninsula and northwest Africa, distinguished from Common Chiffchaff by a distinctly different song — a rhythmic, whistled 'swee-syuu' series rather than the repetitive 'chiff-chaff'. Genetic divergence from P. collybita is considerable. Winters in West Africa.

children.name: Phylloscopus ijimae
children.description: A rare and localised leaf warbler breeding only in the Izu Islands south of Tokyo, Japan, named after the Japanese ornithologist Isokichi Ijima. Listed as Vulnerable due to its very restricted range and threats from introduced predators. Closely related to the Eastern Crowned Warbler.

children.name: Phylloscopus inornatus
children.description: One of the most remarkable vagrant passerines in Europe — a tiny Siberian species that appears in thousands along Atlantic coasts each autumn, far west of its normal wintering range in Southeast Asia. Its thin, high-pitched 'tsweest' call is easily learned. Breeds in taiga from the Urals to northeast Siberia.

children.name: Phylloscopus kansuensis
children.description: A recently described cryptic species from montane forest in Gansu and adjacent provinces of north-central China, separated from Pallas's Leaf Warbler complex by song and genetics. One of several taxa elevated to full species status as Chinese mountain birds receive intensive bioacoustic scrutiny. Resident or altitudinal migrant.

children.name: Phylloscopus laurae
children.description: An African leaf warbler restricted to montane forest and bamboo thickets in the highlands of Democratic Republic of Congo and Rwanda. Named after Laura Chapin Boulton, American ethnomusicologist and naturalist who collected in the region. One of the least-studied members of the family.

children.name: Phylloscopus lorenzii
children.description: A compact, dark leaf warbler endemic to montane forest and scrub in the Caucasus, named after the zoologist Hermann Lorenz. Long treated as a subspecies of Dusky Warbler, it was elevated to species status based on vocal and morphological differences. Winters in northeastern Africa.

children.name: Phylloscopus maculipennis
children.description: A distinctive small Himalayan leaf warbler with a grey-white throat and bold white wingbars on a greenish wing. Breeds in montane forest with thick moss and epiphyte cover from the Himalayas to southwest China. A common and vocal species in mixed-species insectivore flocks in winter.

children.name: Phylloscopus magnirostris
children.description: One of the largest Phylloscopus, with a noticeably heavier bill than its congeners — reflecting its preference for larger invertebrate prey. Breeds in Himalayan mixed forest and adjacent hills; a long-distance migrant wintering in South and Southeast Asia. Its loud, clear song is a feature of mid-altitude forests.

children.name: Phylloscopus maforensis
children.description: An island endemic restricted to Numfor Island in Cenderawasih Bay, Indonesia (West Papua). One of a cluster of small island endemic leaf warblers in Wallacea and the western Pacific, each the product of ancient colonisation events. Resident in lowland and foothill forest; poorly studied.

children.name: Phylloscopus montis
children.description: A widespread montane leaf warbler of Southeast Asian highlands from the Thai-Malay peninsula south through the Philippines and Borneo. Divides into several distinctive island subspecies. Active and vocal in mossy montane forest; often the most abundant bird in canopy-gleaning mixed flocks.

children.name: Phylloscopus neglectus
children.description: One of the plainest members of the genus — wholly unstreaked, uniformly olive-grey — breeding in stony hill country and juniper scrub in Iran, Afghanistan, and Pakistan. Winters on the Indian plains; an early spring migrant returning to its upland breeding grounds. Despite its name, a charming and active small warbler.

children.name: Phylloscopus nitidus
children.description: A bright green leaf warbler with a yellow supercilium, breeding in Caucasian and Turkish montane forests and widely debated as to whether it constitutes a full species or a race of Greenish Warbler. Highly vocal in its montane forest habitat; winters in India. Its relationship to P. trochiloides remains taxonomically contested.

children.name: Phylloscopus occipitalis
children.description: A large crowned leaf warbler of Himalayan and Central Asian mountain forests, with a pale yellow crown stripe and bold wingbars. Breeds in pine and cedar forests from Pakistan to central Nepal; winters in southern India. One of the larger and more robust Phylloscopus species.

children.name: Phylloscopus ogilviegranti
children.description: A crown-striped leaf warbler of montane forests in southwest China and adjacent Southeast Asia, named after Major William Robert Ogilvie-Grant of the Natural History Museum, London. Split from closely related Blyth's Leaf Warbler on the basis of subtle plumage and clear vocal differences. Resident or short-distance migrant.

children.name: Phylloscopus olivaceus
children.description: A dull olive leaf warbler endemic to the Philippines, where it inhabits montane forest from Luzon south to the Visayas. One of the least distinctive members of the genus in appearance, but its song is loud and distinctive. A resident species showing little inclination to migrate beyond its island range.

children.name: Phylloscopus orientalis
children.description: The eastern counterpart of Western Bonelli's Warbler, breeding in montane forests of the Balkans, Turkey, and the Middle East. Almost identical in appearance to P. bonelli but with a subtly different song and reliably separated by its range. Winters in the eastern Sahel zone of sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Phylloscopus plumbeitarsus
children.description: A Greenish Warbler relative of the Siberian taiga, distinguished by two distinct wingbars rather than the single bar of its close relative. A scarce vagrant to Western Europe; closely related to Greenish Warbler but genetically and vocally distinct. Winters in South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Phylloscopus poliocephalus
children.description: A remarkably species-rich complex of island leaf warblers distributed across Wallacea, Melanesia, and the western Pacific — currently recognised as a superspecies with many population-level taxa. Each island group hosts a distinct form with characteristic song and subtle plumage differences. A textbook case of island radiation.

children.name: Phylloscopus proregulus
children.description: A tiny jewel of a bird — yellow rump, bold supercilium, and double wingbars — that turns up in significant numbers on European coasts each autumn after reverse-migrating west from Siberia. Named after Peter Simon Pallas, the German naturalist who described the fauna of the Russian Empire in the 18th century. Breeds in Siberian taiga.

children.name: Phylloscopus pulcher
children.description: One of the more ornate Himalayan leaf warblers, with warm buff wingbars and a rusty-orange rump that make it one of the more instantly identifiable members of the genus. Breeds in Himalayan and Tibetan montane conifer forest at high altitude. A fairly common resident with only short altitudinal movements in winter.

children.name: Phylloscopus reguloides
children.description: A crown-striped, double-winged-barred leaf warbler widely distributed in Himalayan and Chinese montane forest, named after the English zoologist Edward Blyth. One of several visually similar crown-striped Phylloscopus species that have required detailed bioacoustic study to separate. Winters in hills and lowlands across South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Phylloscopus ricketti
children.description: A strikingly yellow leaf warbler of subtropical forests in southern China and Southeast Asia, with a bright sulphur-yellow breast contrasting with its olive-green upperparts. Named after Lieutenant Colonel C. B. Rickett who collected in Fujian, China, in the 1890s. Winters in the lowlands of Indochina and the Malay Peninsula.

children.name: Phylloscopus ruficapilla
children.description: An African leaf warbler of montane forest and forest edges in the mountains of East and Central Africa, with a bright yellow throat contrasting with olive upperparts. Belongs to a clade of African Phylloscopus that colonised the continent independently from Asian ancestors. Common and vocal in Afromontane forest patches.

children.name: Phylloscopus schwarzi
children.description: A large, heavy-bodied leaf warbler of the Siberian taiga with an extremely broad, creamy supercilium and a habit of creeping through dense low scrub. Named after the naturalist Gustav Radde who explored Siberia in the 1850s. Annually recorded as a vagrant in Western Europe; winters in Southeast Asia.

children.name: Phylloscopus sichuanensis
children.description: A recently described cryptic species of broadleaf mountain forest in Sichuan and adjacent provinces of China, described from specimens previously assigned to the Blyth's Leaf Warbler complex. Revealed by bioacoustic surveys in the 2000s; its complex song is markedly different from related taxa. Short-distance migrant.

children.name: Phylloscopus sindianus
children.description: A high-altitude chiffchaff of the western Himalayas and Hindu Kush, breeding at elevations up to 4,000 m in scrub and dwarf juniper. Appears cold and drab compared to collybita, with a pale supercilium and very dark legs. Winters in the lowlands of Pakistan and northwest India; a common altitudinal migrant.

children.name: Phylloscopus subaffinis
children.description: A plain, buff-washed leaf warbler breeding in scrub and secondary forest in central and southern China. For a time treated as a subspecies of Tickell's Leaf Warbler but separated by slight differences in plumage tones and song. Winters across much of Southeast Asia in lowland scrub.

children.name: Phylloscopus subviridis
children.description: A small, greenish leaf warbler of the western Himalayas, named after W. E. Brooks, a 19th-century collector active in Afghanistan and Pakistan. Breeds in high conifer and mixed forest in Afghanistan, Pakistan, and northwest India. Winters in the foothills and lowlands of the northwestern Indian subcontinent.

children.name: Phylloscopus tytleri
children.description: A little-known and rarely observed leaf warbler breeding in high Himalayan and Tibetan shrubland, named after Robert Christopher Tytler, a British army officer and naturalist in India. Its breeding grounds were located only in the late 20th century. Winters in northeastern India and Bangladesh.

children.name: Phylloscopus tristis
children.description: The 'tristis' chiffchaff is a cold, washed-out race — now widely treated as a full species — breeding across Siberia east of the Ural Mountains. Its entirely buff-and-grey plumage, lacking any yellow or green tones, and soft 'peep' call immediately distinguish it from Common Chiffchaff. Increasingly recorded as a vagrant in Western Europe.

children.name: Phylloscopus tenellipes
children.description: An East Asian leaf warbler breeding in mixed and coniferous forests of Siberia, northeast China, and Korea, named for its conspicuously pale pinkish-flesh legs that immediately distinguish it from the otherwise similar Arctic Warbler. Winters in Southeast Asia; a rare vagrant to Western Europe.

children.name: Phylloscopus trivirgatus
children.description: A boldly crown-striped leaf warbler of Southeast Asian and Philippine montane forests, sometimes treated as a subspecies of the Island Leaf Warbler complex. Active and conspicuous in mossy high-altitude forest; often the dominant small passerine in canopy insectivore flocks. Resident across its montane island range.

children.name: Phylloscopus trochiloides
children.description: A key species in the study of ring speciation — the circum-Himalayan subspecies form a genetic ring in which adjacent populations interbreed but the two terminal forms (meeting in central Siberia) are reproductively isolated. An increasingly common migrant through Western Europe. Loud, emphatic song distinguishes it from Yellow-browed Warbler.

children.name: Phylloscopus umbrovirens
children.description: The most widespread African leaf warbler, distributed across montane forest and dense scrub from Eritrea and Ethiopia south to Tanzania. One of the characteristic birds of Afromontane forest; common and often heard before seen. Part of the Afrotropical clade of Phylloscopus that diversified independently in African highlands.

children.name: Phylloscopus xanthoschistos
children.description: A distinctive Himalayan leaf warbler with a slate-grey hood contrasting with bright yellow underparts, common in forest understory from Pakistan to western China. One of the most recognisable members of the family in the Himalayas. Forms single-species roving flocks in the non-breeding season; often very tame.

children.name: Phylloscopus yunnanensis
children.description: A plain, greenish leaf warbler restricted to broadleaf and mixed mountain forests in Yunnan and adjacent provinces of China, recently elevated to species status on vocal grounds. Breeds at moderate montane elevations; winters in lowland southern China and northern Indochina. Part of a complex of cryptic taxa in the region.

children.name: Phylloscopus caucasicus
children.description: A pale chiffchaff of the Caucasus mountains and adjacent Turkey, separated from Common Chiffchaff by subtle vocal differences and a more restricted breeding range. One of several taxa in the P. collybita complex that have been successively elevated to species status as fieldwork and genetics advance. Winters in northeast Africa.

children.name: Phylloscopus examinandus
children.description: A leaf warbler split from the Arctic Warbler complex, breeding on the Kamchatka Peninsula and adjacent Russian Far East. Bioacoustic analysis revealed distinct song structure that differs from the nominate borealis population. Winters in Southeast Asia; a rare vagrant to Western Europe and Alaska.

children.name: Phylloscopus presbytes
children.description: An island endemic of Timor and adjacent Lesser Sundas, representing one of the westernmost representatives of the Pacific island leaf warbler radiation. Resident in montane and foothill forest; one of the most poorly studied members of the family due to the logistical challenges of fieldwork in its range.

children.name: Phylloscopus herberti
children.description: An African leaf warbler with an unusual black cap, found in montane forest from Cameroon to western Uganda. Part of the diverse clade of African Phylloscopus that colonised the continent from Asia. Named after Sir Robert Herbert, Under-Secretary of State for the Colonies in the 19th century.

children.name: Phylloscopus budongoensis
children.description: A recently described African leaf warbler from Budongo Forest and adjacent highland forests in Uganda and Rwanda. Separated from the Brown Woodland Warbler on vocal and morphological grounds following surveys in the 2000s. One of the clearest examples of cryptic species diversity within the Afrotropical Phylloscopus clade.

children.name: Phylloscopus laetus
children.description: A distinctive African leaf warbler with reddish-tinged facial sides, restricted to mountain forest in the Albertine Rift from Rwanda to eastern DRC. One of the most colourful members of the African Phylloscopus clade. An uncommon forest-interior species with a restricted range that makes it a target for birders visiting the region.

children.name: Phylloscopus neumanni
children.description: An African leaf warbler of highland forests in Cameroon and Nigeria, named after the German ornithologist Oscar Neumann who collected extensively in Africa in the late 19th century. Part of a complex of Afromontane Phylloscopus whose relationships have only recently been clarified through genetic studies.

children.name: Phylloscopus davisoni
children.description: A crown-striped leaf warbler of montane forests in the hills of Myanmar, Thailand, and adjacent southwest China, named after the naturalist W. R. D. Davison. Has prominent white outer tail feathers which flash as it moves through forest undergrowth. Forms part of a complex of similar crowned warblers whose species limits are debated.

children.name: Phylloscopus goodsoni
children.description: A small, active leaf warbler of subtropical broadleaf forest in southern China and adjacent Indochina, named after Arthur Goodson who collected the type specimen in Guangdong. A member of the Phylloscopus reguloides complex that was elevated on the basis of vocal and morphological differences.

children.name: Phylloscopus calciatilis
children.description: A leaf warbler discovered in 2009 in northern Laos and adjacent Vietnam, specialised in its preference for scrub and secondary forest on karst limestone outcrops. One of the most recently described Phylloscopus species; its striking habitat specificity makes it one of the most ecologically unusual members of the genus.

children.name: Phylloscopus amoenus
children.description: A leaf warbler endemic to Kolombangara (Kulambangra) Island in the Solomon Islands, representing the far eastern extent of Phylloscopus range in the Pacific. A forest-interior species of montane and foothill forest; poorly studied due to the island's remote location and limited access.

children.name: Phylloscopus rotiensis
children.description: A tiny leaf warbler endemic to Rote Island (Roti), the southernmost island of Indonesia, described as a new species in 2020 following fieldwork by a joint Indonesian–Australian team. One of the most recently named Phylloscopus species; its restricted range and small island habitat raise immediate conservation concerns. Resident in lowland and hillside forest.

children.name: Phylloscopus valentini
children.description: A plain-looking leaf warbler of high-altitude scrub in Gansu and Qinghai, China, named after the botanist Gustav Valentin. Long treated as a race within Blyth's Leaf Warbler complex; molecular evidence supported species status. Resident or altitudinal migrant wintering in the lowlands of south-central China.

children.name: Seicercus
children.description: Flycatcher-warblers — a genus of small, often colourful leaf warblers formerly placed in Seicercus but now absorbed into Phylloscopidae. Distinguished from typical Phylloscopus by broader bills, shorter wings, and a tendency to flycatch insects on the wing as well as glean from foliage. The group reaches its greatest diversity in the Himalayas and Chinese mountain forests.

children.name: Seicercus affinis
children.description: A colourful flycatcher-warbler of Himalayan and Southeast Asian montane forest, with a distinctive white eye-ring that gives the species its name. Forages actively in the forest understorey, often flycatching short sallies from a perch. Common in mixed-species insectivore flocks throughout its montane range.

children.name: Seicercus burkii
children.description: A Himalayan flycatcher-warbler with a bright green crown stripe bordered by grey lateral stripes, yellow underparts, and conspicuous white eye-ring. Named after Colonel James Burke, an Irish officer in the East India Company who collected birds in India. One of the most frequently encountered species in Himalayan understorey flocks.

children.name: Seicercus castaniceps
children.description: A spectacularly patterned flycatcher-warbler with a rich chestnut crown, grey sides to the head, and yellow underparts. Widespread in montane forests from the eastern Himalayas to southern China and the Malay Peninsula. An abundant species in mixed-species flocks; highly active and easier to hear than to observe.

children.name: Seicercus grammiceps
children.description: A montane flycatcher-warbler of Sundaland, resident in highland forest on the Greater Sunda Islands — Sumatra, Java, and Borneo. Like other insular Seicercus, it is resident and shows little migratory behaviour. Active in canopy and sub-canopy of mossy montane forest; regularly joins mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Seicercus montis
children.description: A bright yellow-underparts flycatcher-warbler of montane forests from the Thai-Malay Peninsula east through Borneo and the Philippines. One of the most widespread Seicercus in Southeast Asia; often found with other Phylloscopidae species in mixed montane flocks. Resident throughout its range with only minor altitudinal movements.

children.name: Seicercus omeiensis
children.description: A little-known flycatcher-warbler described from specimens taken on Mt. Emei in Sichuan — one of several taxa isolated on the mountains of central China. Only recently widely recognised as a full species following intensive fieldwork on Chinese montane birds. A short-distance migrant with a limited breeding range.

children.name: Seicercus poliogenys
children.description: A Himalayan flycatcher-warbler with plain grey cheeks contrasting with its yellow-washed underparts and greenish upperparts. Breeds in dense broadleaf forest understorey in the eastern Himalayas and southwest China. Less commonly encountered than many congeners; tends to stay low in dense shaded ravines.

children.name: Seicercus soror
children.description: A Chinese flycatcher-warbler of montane broadleaf forest in south-central and southeastern China, closely related to and formerly confused with the Grey-cheeked Warbler. Differences in song and genetics support its treatment as a separate species. Winters in the lowlands of southern China and northern Vietnam.

children.name: Seicercus tephrocephalus
children.description: A flycatcher-warbler with a diagnostic plain grey crown and no crown stripe, separating it from most other Seicercus. Breeds in broadleaf montane forest in the eastern Himalayas and southwest China. An active and vocal species, usually detected by its distinctive song before being seen in the forest understorey.

children.name: Seicercus whistleri
children.description: A Himalayan flycatcher-warbler named after the British ornithologist Hugh Whistler, who produced a landmark handbook on Indian birds. One of the Seicercus species with a prominent grey crown and distinctive song, breeding in Himalayan temperate broadleaf and mixed forest. Winters in foothills and adjacent plains.
